content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 100r–101v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_003_page_001.jpg, DEU_003_page_002.jpg, DEU_003_page_003.jpg; source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_003_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_003_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** "Next we turned and went up the road to Bashan. Og king of Bashan came out with all his forces to meet us in battle at Edrei. **2** The LORD said to me: 'Do not fear him, for I have handed him over to you along with all his forces and his land. Do to him what you did to Sihon king of the Amorites who reigned in Heshbon.'

**3** "So the LORD our God also handed Og king of Bashan over to us, together with all his forces. We struck him down until no survivor was left. **4** At that time we captured all his cities — there was not a single town we did not take from him: sixty cities, the entire region of Argob — the kingdom of Og in Bashan. **5** All these were fortified cities with high walls, gates, and bars — in addition to a great many open villages. **6** We completely destroyed them, as we had done with Sihon king of Heshbon — every city, men, women, and children. **7** But all the livestock and the plunder from the cities we kept as spoil for ourselves.

**8** "At that time we seized from the two Amorite kings the territory on the east side of the Jordan, from the Arnon Gorge to Mount Hermon. **9** (Sidonians call Hermon Sirion, while the Amorites call it Senir.) **10** We took all the towns of the plateau, all Gilead, and all Bashan as far as Salecah and Edrei — towns of the kingdom of Og in Bashan. **11** (For the record: only Og king of Bashan was left of the remaining Rephaim. His bed was made of iron. It is in Rabbah of the Ammonites to this day — nine cubits long and four cubits wide, by the common cubit.)

**12** "At that time, out of the territory that had been ours — the territory north of Aroer, which lies by the Arnon Gorge, with half the hill country of Gilead and its towns — I gave it to the Reubenites and the Gadites. **13** The rest of Gilead and all of Bashan, the kingdom of Og, I gave to the half-tribe of Manasseh. (The whole region of Argob in Bashan used to be called the land of the Rephaim. **14** Jair, a Manassehite, took the whole region of Argob as far as the border of Geshur and Maacah, and named them after himself — Havvoth-jair — as they are called to this day.) **15** I gave Gilead to Machir. **16** And to the Reubenites and the Gadites I gave the territory from Gilead to the Arnon Gorge — the middle of the gorge as the border — all the way to the Jabbok River, which is the border with the Ammonites. **17** Along with the Arabah and the Jordan as the border, from Kinnereth down to the Sea of the Arabah — that is, the Salt Sea — below the slopes of Pisgah to the east.

**18** "At that time I commanded you: 'The LORD your God has given you this land to possess. All your fighting men are to cross over armed ahead of your fellow Israelites, the sons of Israel. **19** Only your wives, your children, and your livestock — I know you have much livestock — may stay in the towns I have given you **20** until the LORD gives rest to your fellow Israelites as he has given it to you, and they too have taken possession of the land the LORD your God is giving them across the Jordan. After that you may each return to your own possession that I have given you.'

**21** "At that time I also commanded Joshua: 'Your own eyes have seen everything the LORD your God has done to these two kings. The LORD will do the same to all the kingdoms you are about to cross into. **22** Do not be afraid of them — the LORD your God himself will fight for you.'

**23** "At that time I pleaded with the LORD: **24** 'O Sovereign LORD, you have begun to show your servant your greatness and your powerful hand. What god is there in heaven or on earth who can do the deeds and mighty works you do? **25** Let me cross over and see that good land beyond the Jordan — that beautiful hill country and Lebanon!'

**26** "But the LORD was angry with me because of you and would not listen to me. The LORD said to me: 'Enough! Do not speak to me again about this matter. **27** Go up to the top of Pisgah and look west and north and south and east. Look at it with your own eyes, for you will not cross this Jordan. **28** But commission Joshua, strengthen him and encourage him — because he is the one who will lead this people across, and he will cause them to inherit the land you will see.'

**29** "So we stayed in the valley opposite Beth-peor."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Dynamic choices — PNEUMA:**

**V.4 — "sixty cities, the entire region of Argob":** The detail is military and administrative — Og's kingdom was vast. PNEUMA preserves the catalogue because its scale is part of the theological claim: no power, however entrenched, can withstand the LORD.

**V.11 — "his bed was made of iron...nine cubits long":** The parenthetical about Og's iron bed is a parenthetical marveling at his size — Og was remembered as a giant. PNEUMA keeps the anthropological note intact. A cubit was roughly 18 inches, making Og's bed about 13.5 feet — the text invites astonishment.

**V.24 — "O Sovereign LORD":** Hebrew Adonai YHWH — the doubled address, combining the title of absolute lordship with the personal covenant name. PNEUMA's "O Sovereign LORD" captures the urgency and intimacy of Moses' prayer without creating confusion between the two divine names.

**V.25 — "that beautiful hill country and Lebanon":** Moses' longing is personal and poignant — he uses the demonstrative "that" (ha-tov ha-zeh) with something almost like yearning. PNEUMA's "that beautiful hill country" gives the geography an emotional weight that mirrors Moses' desire.

**V.26 — "Enough! Do not speak to me again about this matter":** God's refusal is abrupt and final — Hebrew rav lakh al tosef daber elai ba-davar hazeh — "enough for you; do not add to speaking to me about this thing." PNEUMA renders the bluntness without softening. This is one of the most human moments in the entire Torah: Moses denied. God unwilling to discuss it further.

**V.27 — "Look at it with your own eyes":** The gift within the judgment: Moses may see, though he may not enter. PNEUMA preserves this as consolation — the vision is given even as the crossing is forbidden.

**Vaticanus (LXX) observation:** Vaticanus page 145–146 covers this chapter. The LXX at v.11 renders Og's bed as a "kline sidere" — iron couch/bed — identical concept. Notably, the LXX specifies the measurement system as "en pechei andros" — "in a man's cubit" — corresponding to the MT's "be-ammah ish." At v.24 the LXX begins "Kyrie kyrie" — "Lord, Lord" — a doubled vocative that in Greek carries enormous weight and would echo through the NT's use of the same phrase in moments of desperate appeal. This double vocative is visible in the Vaticanus manuscript and is a distinctive textual marker.
